text: So, one after another, either as pilots or passengers, will the members of the club ascend; and before the sheds are closed and the aerodrome deserted, each and all will have soared in flight, and tasted that thrill and exultation which comes of a rush in a plane through the cool of the evening air. But today, if we try to grasp such a notion as this, our state of mind is very like that of our grandfathers had some prophet dared tell them the day would dawn when, seated comfortably at dinner in a car on wheels, men would be drawn by an engine at 60 miles an hour: and yet the man or woman who has not, say twenty years hence, made a journey through the air, will be in exactly the same position as one who, at the present time, has never been by train.
author: Claude Grahame-White

didItHoldUp.analysis: Grahame-White, writing just 11 years after the Wright brothers' first flight, predicted that within 20 years air travel would be as normal as rail travel. By 1934, commercial aviation was growing rapidly — Imperial Airways, Pan Am, and Lufthansa were connecting continents. By the jet age of the 1960s, his vision was fully realized. What makes this passage beautiful is the rhetorical move: he compares the incredulity of his readers to that of their grandparents facing trains. Each generation struggles to believe in the next revolution, even as they live comfortably with the last one. The pattern holds today — replace 'aeroplane' with 'autonomous vehicle' and the sentiment is identical.
